commands.description: List directory contents. Often used with flags to see hidden files or file details.
commands.mistake: Running ls in a folder with millions of files without pagination or limit, causing the terminal output buffer to freeze.

commands.description: Change the shell working directory to a specified path.
commands.example:
cd /var/www/html
cd .. # Go up one level
cd ~ # Go to home directory
commands.mistake: Using 'cd' inside an automated shell script without checking if the target directory exists, leading to subsequent commands executing in the wrong folder.

commands.description: Print name of current/working directory.
commands.mistake: Confusing the current working directory in script execution with the directory where the script file is located.

commands.description: Create one or more new directories.
commands.example: mkdir -p project/src/components # Creates nested folders if they do not exist
commands.mistake: Attempting to create nested directories without the '-p' flag, resulting in a 'No such file or directory' error.

commands.description: Copy files and directories from source to destination.
commands.example:
cp config.env.example config.env
cp -R src/ backup/src_backup/ # Recursive copy
commands.mistake: Forgetting the '-R' or '-r' flag when copying directories, which silently ignores folders or throws an error.

commands.description: Move or rename files and directories.
commands.mistake: Accidentally overwriting an existing file at the destination. Use the '-n' flag to prevent overwriting or '-i' for interactive confirmation.

commands.description: Remove files or directories. Use recursive flag to delete folders.
commands.mistake: Running 'rm -rf /' or omitting path configurations, which can permanently wipe out the system filesystem.

commands.description: Search for files in a directory hierarchy based on names, types, sizes, or dates.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to wrap the name pattern in quotes, which causes the shell to expand the glob pattern locally before running find.

commands.description: Change the access permissions of files or directories (read, write, execute).
commands.mistake: Setting 'chmod 777' on files in production, which gives global write and execute access, creating severe security vulnerabilities.

commands.description: Change file owner and group ownership of files and directories.
commands.mistake: Forgetting the -R flag when you want to change ownership for all nested files and subdirectories.

commands.description: Redirect command standard output (stdout) or standard error (stderr) to a file or stream.
commands.mistake: Using '>' when you mean to append. '>' will overwrite the file entirely, destroying previous logs. Use '>>' to append.

commands.description: Build and execute command lines from standard input (useful for chaining output files to arguments).
commands.mistake: Forgetting that xargs splits on spaces/newlines, which can fail or cause issues if filenames contain spaces. Use 'find -print0 | xargs -0' to solve this.

commands.description: Report a snapshot of current running processes in the system.
commands.mistake: Running ps without proper flags and assuming a process isn't running, when it's actually running under a different user or session.

commands.description: Send a signal to a process (usually to terminate or kill it by PID).
commands.mistake: Using 'kill -9' (SIGKILL) immediately. Always try 'kill -15' (SIGTERM) first to allow the process to clean up connection resources and write files.

commands.description: Run a command immune to hangups, allowing background processes to continue running even after you log out of the SSH session.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to add '&' at the end, which keeps the process in the foreground instead of putting it into the background.

commands.description: Interactive, real-time process monitoring display of system activity and resource consumption.
commands.mistake: Leaving high-frequency monitoring tools like top running in multiple ssh terminals, which itself consumes non-trivial CPU cycles.

commands.description: Search text for lines matching a pattern. Supports regular expressions.
commands.mistake: Searching large binary files or node_modules directories without excluding them, resulting in massive terminal outputs.

commands.description: Stream editor for filtering and transforming text (e.g. search and replace).
commands.mistake: Using '-i' (in-place modification) without first running sed without '-i' to preview and verify changes.

commands.description: A versatile pattern scanning and processing language, highly useful for extracting columns from logs/command outputs.
commands.example: ps aux | awk '{print $2, $11}' # Prints PID and command columns
commands.mistake: Confusing field separators (default is whitespace) when parsing CSV files without specifying '-F ","'.

commands.description: Concatenate and display file contents in the terminal.
commands.mistake: Using 'cat' to view extremely large files (e.g., gigabyte log files), which floods the terminal buffer. Use 'less' or 'tail' instead.

commands.description: Output the last part of files. Often used to watch logs in real-time.
commands.example: tail -f -n 100 /var/log/syslog # Follow logs as they grow
commands.mistake: Running 'tail -f' on log-rotated files; if the target file is renamed (rotated), tail might stop displaying new lines. Use '--follow=name' if supported.

commands.description: Output the first part (lines) of files.
commands.mistake: Omitting the number of lines when you want to view a specific amount (default is 10 lines).

commands.description: Report filesystem disk space usage for all mounted drives.
commands.mistake: Ignoring disk space consumption alerts and failing to inspect inode exhaustion ('df -i'), which can freeze systems even if disk space is free.

commands.description: Estimate file space usage recursively for directories.
commands.example: du -sh * # Show total size of each file/folder in current directory
commands.mistake: Running raw 'du' on the root directory `/` without limiting depth or using '-h', causing endless screens of details.

commands.description: Display amount of free and used physical memory (RAM) and swap in the system.
commands.mistake: Misinterpreting the 'available' memory. Linux utilizes unused RAM for buffers/cache; look at 'available' rather than 'free' to gauge actual capacity.

commands.description: Print system kernel, OS, and machine architecture details.
commands.mistake: Assuming all architectures are the same and downloading incompatible binaries (e.g. x86_64 vs arm64).

commands.description: Transfer data from or to a server using supported protocols (HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, etc.).
commands.example:
curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"key":"val"}' https://api.example.com
curl -o app.tar.gz https://example.com/app.tar.gz
commands.mistake: Running curl on HTTPS sites with expired/invalid certificates using '-k' (insecure) flag in production environments, exposing traffic to MITM attacks.

commands.description: Non-interactive network downloader. Great for downloading files recursively.
commands.mistake: Using wget to pull files without limiting retry counts or timeouts on flaky networks, resulting in hung automation scripts.

commands.description: Send ICMP Echo requests to verify host connectivity.
commands.example: ping -c 4 google.com # Stop after sending 4 packets
commands.mistake: Running ping without pack count limit on Linux systems, which will ping infinitely until manually interrupted with Ctrl+C.

commands.description: Open a secure cryptographic network protocol for operating network services securely over an unsecured network.
commands.mistake: Leaving default port 22 exposed to the public internet without SSH key-only authentication, inviting brute force botnets.

commands.description: Securely copy files or directories between a local and remote host or between two remote hosts.
commands.mistake: Using scp recursively ('-r') for directories containing symlinks; it may result in infinite loops or duplicate copies. Use tar over ssh instead.

commands.description: Archiving utility to bundle multiple files into a single archive file (often compressed).
commands.example:
tar -czvf archive.tar.gz ./folder # Compress
tar -xzvf archive.tar.gz # Decompress
commands.mistake: Forgetting to specify the '-f' (file) flag at the very end of options (e.g. doing 'tar -cfzv'), which causes tar to try to write to a file named 'z'.

commands.description: Package and compress or extract zip format archives.
commands.mistake: Running zip without the '-r' flag on folders, resulting in an empty zip archive that contains only the parent directory metadata.
